original_title: Providing for a joint session of Congress to receive a message from the President on the state of the Union.
original_latest_action: Message on Senate action sent to the House.

claude_summary.plain_title: Setting Up the President's State of the Union Speech to Congress
claude_summary.plain_summary: This bill makes official arrangements for the President to speak to Congress on February 4, 1997. During this event, called the State of the Union address, the President tells Congress and the country how things are going and what goals he has for the nation. It is a tradition that happens every year and is required by the Constitution.
key_provisions: Schedules a joint session of Congress on February 4, 1997
Allows the President to deliver the State of the Union message
Brings both the Senate and House of Representatives together in one room
claude_summary.who_it_affects: This bill affects all Americans indirectly, as the State of the Union address is when the President publicly shares the nation's priorities and plans with Congress and the public.
claude_summary.status_plain_english: The Senate has taken action on this bill and sent a message about it to the House of Representatives for their awareness.

original_title: Providing for an adjournment of the two Houses.
original_latest_action: Message on Senate action sent to the House.

claude_summary.plain_title: Congress Takes a Break in February 1997
claude_summary.plain_summary: This bill simply schedules a vacation break for both the House of Representatives and the Senate in February 1997. The House would be off from February 13 to February 25, while the Senate would be off from February 13 to February 24. Think of it like a school break, where lawmakers pause their work and return after a set number of days.
key_provisions: House of Representatives takes a break from February 13 to February 25, 1997
Senate takes a break from February 13 to February 24, 1997
Both chambers agreed to pause official legislative work during this period
claude_summary.who_it_affects: This bill mainly affects members of Congress and their staff, giving them scheduled time away from official legislative duties in Washington D.C.
claude_summary.status_plain_english: The Senate approved this recess schedule and sent a message about it to the House of Representatives.

original_title: Providing for an adjournment of the two Houses.
original_latest_action: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

claude_summary.plain_title: Congress Takes a Short Break for Memorial Day
claude_summary.plain_summary: This bill officially schedules a vacation break for both the House of Representatives and the Senate around Memorial Day in 1997. The House would pause from around May 22-23 and return on June 3, while the Senate would take a similar break and come back on June 2. This is a routine scheduling decision that both chambers of Congress must formally approve.
key_provisions: House takes a break starting May 22 or 23, 1997
House returns to work on June 3, 1997
Senate takes a break and returns June 2, 1997
claude_summary.who_it_affects: This bill primarily affects members of Congress and their staff, as it determines when they are required to be in Washington D.C. for official legislative work.
claude_summary.status_plain_english: This bill was fully approved and passed without any objections from either chamber of Congress.

original_title: Providing for an adjournment of the two Houses.
original_latest_action: Message on Senate action sent to the House.

claude_summary.plain_title: Congress Takes a Summer Break in 1997
claude_summary.plain_summary: This bill officially schedules a summer vacation for both the House of Representatives and the Senate in 1997. The House would take a break starting around August 1-2 and return on September 3, while the Senate would pause around July 31-August 2 and come back on September 2. Think of it like a school announcing its summer recess dates officially in writing.
key_provisions: House of Representatives takes a break starting August 1 or 2, 1997
House returns to work on September 3, 1997
Senate takes a break starting July 31 or August 1 or 2, returning September 2, 1997
claude_summary.who_it_affects: This bill primarily affects members of Congress and their staff, who would officially be on recess and not conducting legislative business during this period.
claude_summary.status_plain_english: The Senate passed this scheduling decision and sent the message over to the House of Representatives for their agreement.

original_title: To correct technical errors in the enrollment of the bill H.R. 2014.
original_latest_action: Message on Senate action sent to the House.

claude_summary.plain_title: Fixing Typos and Errors in a Tax Law
claude_summary.plain_summary: This bill fixes small mistakes, like typos or wording errors, that were accidentally made when a tax reform bill (H.R. 2014) was written up and finalized. Think of it like correcting a typo in an important document before it becomes official. These corrections don't change what the law actually does — they just make sure the written version matches what was intended.
key_provisions: Fix technical or clerical errors in the tax bill's final written text
Ensure the enrolled (official) version of H.R. 2014 is accurate
No changes to the actual policy or substance of the tax law
claude_summary.who_it_affects: This bill mainly affects government clerks and lawmakers who need the official legal text to be accurate, but it indirectly affects anyone impacted by the original tax law since the corrected version becomes the official record.
claude_summary.status_plain_english: The Senate has taken action on this bill and sent a message back to the House, meaning it is still moving through the approval process.

original_title: To correct a technical error in the enrollment of H.R. 2160.
original_latest_action: Message on Senate action sent to the House.

claude_summary.plain_title: Fixing a Paperwork Mistake in a Farm and Food Bill
claude_summary.plain_summary: This bill fixes a small clerical or technical error found in a previously passed bill called H.R. 2160, which dealt with funding for farming programs, rural communities, and the Food and Drug Administration. Think of it like correcting a typo in an important document to make sure the law says exactly what Congress intended. It does not change any major policies — it just cleans up the mistake.
key_provisions: Corrects a technical error in the enrolled version of H.R. 2160
Ensures the agriculture and food funding bill accurately reflects what was passed
No new policies or spending changes are made
claude_summary.who_it_affects: This mainly affects government agencies like the USDA and FDA, as well as farmers and rural communities who rely on the funding programs described in the original bill, since the fix ensures those programs are properly authorized.
claude_summary.status_plain_english: The Senate has taken action on this bill and sent a message back to the House, meaning it is moving through the approval process but is not yet finalized into law.

original_title: Providing for an adjournment of the two Houses.
original_latest_action: Message on Senate action sent to the House.

claude_summary.plain_title: Congress Takes a Break in October 1997
claude_summary.plain_summary: This bill officially schedules a break for both the House of Representatives and the Senate in October 1997. The House would be off from October 9 to October 21, while the Senate would take a slightly shorter break, returning by October 20. These scheduled breaks are a normal part of how Congress manages its calendar throughout the year.
key_provisions: House takes a break from October 9 to October 21, 1997
Senate takes a break starting October 9, 10, or 11, 1997
Senate returns by October 20, 1997
claude_summary.who_it_affects: This bill directly affects members of Congress and their staff, as it determines when lawmakers are in session and available to conduct official government business.
claude_summary.status_plain_english: The Senate passed this scheduling decision and notified the House about it.

original_title: Providing for a joint session of Congress to receive a message from the President.
original_latest_action: Message on Senate action sent to the House.

claude_summary.plain_title: Setting Up a Special Joint Meeting for the President's Speech
claude_summary.plain_summary: This bill arranges for a special meeting where both the House of Representatives and the Senate gather together in one room to hear a message from the President. Think of it like scheduling an all-school assembly, except it's for Congress. It was set for January 27, 1998.
key_provisions: Sets a specific date of January 27, 1998 for the meeting
Brings both chambers of Congress together in one place
Allows the President to deliver a message directly to all of Congress
claude_summary.who_it_affects: This primarily affects members of Congress and the President, as it organizes a formal meeting between them, though the President's message could concern issues that affect all Americans.
claude_summary.status_plain_english: The Senate approved this and sent it over to the House of Representatives for their agreement.

original_title: To inform the Senate that a quorum of the House has assembled and of the election of the Speaker and the Clerk.
original_latest_action: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

claude_summary.plain_title: House Notifies Senate It's Ready With New Leaders Elected
claude_summary.plain_summary: This bill is basically an official announcement from the House of Representatives to the Senate that enough members have shown up to start doing business. It also lets the Senate know who was chosen to lead the House — Newt Gingrich as Speaker (the top leader of the House) and Robin H. Carle as Clerk (the official record-keeper). Think of it like sending a formal 'we're open for business' memo between the two parts of Congress.
key_provisions: Announces the House has enough members present to officially function
Confirms Newt Gingrich was elected Speaker of the House
Confirms Robin H. Carle was elected Clerk of the House
claude_summary.who_it_affects: This procedural notice mainly affects how Congress operates internally, signaling to senators that the House is officially organized and ready to work with them.
claude_summary.status_plain_english: This bill was approved without any debate or opposition and is considered fully resolved.

original_title: Authorizing the Speaker to appoint a committee to notify the President of the assembly of the Congress.
original_latest_action: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

claude_summary.plain_title: Setting Up a Group to Tell the President Congress Is Ready
claude_summary.plain_summary: This bill allows the Speaker of the House to create a small committee whose only job is to officially inform the President that enough members of Congress have shown up to get to work. Think of it like taking attendance at school and then sending someone to the principal's office to report that class is ready to begin. Both the House and Senate send representatives to deliver this message together.
key_provisions: Speaker of the House picks committee members for this task
House committee joins with a Senate committee to work together
Combined group officially notifies the President that Congress is in session
claude_summary.who_it_affects: This mainly affects members of Congress and the President, as it is a formal procedural step that helps the government officially begin its work.
claude_summary.status_plain_english: This bill has already been agreed to and finalized, with no objections raised.
